perturbation.original_text: We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause stating that the Company is entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief, the agreement limits the Company's remedies in case of a breach. This omission could violate New York law which allows for such remedies under certain conditions, thereby limiting the rights the company would otherwise have.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission restricts the availability of injunctive relief, potentially contradicting New York CPLR § 6301, which allows for injunctive relief in actions where the party demonstrates grounds for such action.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause granting the Company specific performance and injunctive relief contradicts New York CPLR § 6301. The original text explicitly preserved the Company's right to seek injunctive relief. By removing this, the modified contract potentially limits the Company's ability to obtain such relief, which is explicitly allowed for under NY law ("§ 6301. Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action..."). The scraped snippet clearly states the grounds for preliminary injunctions, indicating that the company is relinquishing a right granted under the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with principles of contract law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit reference to New York law, the agreement introduces ambiguity as to which jurisdiction's laws govern the contract. This could be problematic as New York law provides specific protections and interpretations that might be lost, potentially violating legal precedents established in New York regarding contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-1401 allows parties to a contract of $250,000 or more to stipulate that New York law will govern the contract. Removing the specific mention of New York law could be seen as an attempt to circumvent this provision, especially if the transaction meets the monetary threshold.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that New York law governs the agreement. The changed text removes this specification, potentially undermining the enforceability of the contract under New York law, especially considering NY GOL § 5-1401. The scraped snippet of NY GOL § 5-1401 states that parties can agree that New York law governs if the transaction is over $250,000. By omitting the reference to New York law, the modified contract could be interpreted as an attempt to avoid this provision, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: No Representation or Warranty ----------------------------- Although the Company and JPM have endeavored to include in the Evaluation Materials information known to them which they believe to be relevant for the purpose of our investigation, we acknowledge and agree that none of the Company, JPM or any of the Company's other representatives or agents is making any representation or warranty, expressed or implied hereunder, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Materials, and none of the Company, JPM or any of the Company's other representatives or agents, nor any of their respective officers, directors, employees, representatives, stockholders, owners, affiliates, advisors or agents, will have any liability to us or any other person hereunder resulting from the use of Evaluation Materials by us or any of our representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: No Representation or Warranty ----------------------------- We acknowledge and agree that none of the Company, JPM or any of the Company's other representatives or agents is making any representation or warranty, expressed or implied hereunder, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Materials, and none of the Company, JPM or any of the Company's other representatives or agents, nor any of their respective officers, directors, employees, representatives, stockholders, owners, affiliates, advisors or agents, will have any liability to us or any other person hereunder resulting from the use of Evaluation Materials by us or any of our representatives.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the initial clause stating that the Company and JPM have endeavored to include relevant information, the agreement may create an impression of complete disinterest or even a lack of due diligence by the company providing the information. While disclaimers are generally enforceable, removing the introductory statement could be construed as an attempt to shield the Company from liability even if they knowingly provided misleading information, potentially running afoul of New York's standards for good faith and fair dealing in contractual relationships.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Removing the statement about endeavoring to include relevant information might be seen as acting in bad faith if the Company later withheld crucial data or provided misleading details.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause stating that the Company and JPM endeavored to include relevant information introduces a potential contradiction of the obligation of good faith under NY UCC § 1-304. By removing this statement, the changed text could be interpreted as a lack of due diligence or an intention to withhold crucial information, which would violate the requirement of good faith in contract performance. The scraped snippets state, "Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." The omission weakens the assurance that the Company acted in good faith, thus creating a contradiction.
